text: After completing the sleeve gastrectomy, the camera is repositioned to the peri-umbilical trocar, and the assistant operates through the left lower quadrant trocar. The surgeon identifies the pylorus and marks it with a suture to maintain visibility during dissection. Using a vessel sealer, the surgeon carefully creates a tunnel in a retro duodenal fashion, taking down fine adhesions while avoiding the pancreas. The peritoneum is freed and mobilized in the supraduodenal area, allowing access to the tunnel underneath, where a Penrose drain is utilized.

The surgeon uses a blue load cartridge with buttressing material on the inferior component to transect the duodenal bulb approximately two to three centimeters from the pylorus. The duodenal bulb is connected to the ileum, with the proximal part of the small bowel marked by a white suture. A duodenotomy is performed, and an opening is made in the ileum using a vessel sealer to enlarge the openings for a comfortable anastomosis. Two pieces of Vicryl suture of different colors are tied together and run in a traditional manner for the posterior row, with canal stitches around the corners.

The surgeon runs the ileum towards the distal end, encountering sutures at the 120-centimeter mark, where the ileo-ileal anastomosis is performed using a white cartridge stapler from the left mid-abdomen. The common enterotomy is closed with a running suture, and the mesenteric defect is closed to minimize the risk of an internal hernia. The small bowel is divided distal to the ileo-duodenostomy, forming the Roux-en-Y configuration of the biliopancreatic diversion. The anastomosis is tested by injecting air through the Visi-G tube while submerged under saline.
